questions.clue: A nymph punished by Hera with the inability to speak first gave her name to the acoustic phenomenon of reflected sound.

citations.title: Echo (mythology)
citations.snippet: In Greek mythology, Echo was an Oread who resided on Mount Cithaeron. Zeus loved consorting with beautiful nymphs and often visited them on Earth. Eventually, Zeus's wife, Hera, became suspicious, and came from Mount Olympus in an attempt t

questions.clue: This English word for noisy chaos takes its name from a historic London psychiatric hospital formally known as Bethlem Royal Hospital.

citations.title: Bethlem Royal Hospital
citations.snippet: Bethlem Royal Hospital, also known as St Mary Bethlehem, Bethlehem Hospital and Bedlam, is a psychiatric hospital in Bromley, London. Its famous history has inspired several horror books, films, and television series, most notably Bedlam, a

questions.clue: This word for a substance said to arouse desire takes its name from the ancient Greek goddess associated with love, beauty, and passion.

citations.title: Aphrodite
citations.snippet: Aphrodite is an ancient Greek goddess associated with love, lust, beauty, pleasure, passion, procreation, and as her syncretised Roman counterpart Venus, desire, sex, fertility, prosperity, and victory. Aphrodite's major symbols include sea

questions.clue: This addictive alkaloid's name traces back to a 16th-century French diplomat who sent tobacco seeds to Paris in 1560 and had them promoted to the royal court as a medicine.

citations.title: Jean Nicot
citations.snippet: Jean Nicot de Villemain was a French diplomat and scholar. He is famous for being the first to bring tobacco to France, including snuff tobacco. Nicotine is named after the tobacco plant Nicotiana tabacum, which in turn is named after Jean

questions.clue: This term for an independently-minded nonconformist traces to a 19th-century Texas rancher who signed his state's Declaration of Independence and famously left his cattle unbranded.

citations.title: Samuel Maverick
citations.snippet: Samuel Augustus Maverick was a Texas lawyer, politician, land baron and signer of the Texas Declaration of Independence. His name is the source of the term "maverick", first attested in 1867. He was the grandfather of Texas politician Maury

questions.clue: Coined by the English author Horace Walpole in 1754, this word describes the making of a fortunate discovery entirely by chance.

citations.title: Serendipity
citations.snippet: Serendipity is an unplanned fortunate discovery. The term was coined by Horace Walpole in 1754.

questions.clue: This Latin phrase, meaning 'my fault' or 'my mistake,' serves as a formal acknowledgment of personal wrongdoing and in religious contexts may be accompanied by beating the breast.

citations.title: Mea culpa
citations.snippet: Mea culpa is a phrase of Latin origin that means my fault or my mistake, and is an acknowledgment of having done wrong. The expression is also used as an admission of having made a mistake that should have been avoided and, in a religious c

questions.clue: This Latin phrase, literally meaning 'for this,' describes solutions designed for a specific situation rather than as a generalized approach.

citations.title: Ad hoc
citations.snippet: Ad hoc is a Latin phrase meaning literally 'for this'. In English, it typically signifies a solution designed for a specific purpose, problem, or task rather than a generalized solution adaptable to collateral instances.

questions.clue: This Shakespearean aphorism, with roots traced to at least the 12th century, cautions that not everything that appears valuable or true truly proves to be so.
questions.answer: All that glitters is not gold
aliases: all that glisters is not gold
not all that glitters is gold

citations.title: All that glitters is not gold
citations.snippet: "All that glitters is not gold" is an aphorism stating that not everything that looks precious or true turns out to be so.

questions.clue: This Latin phrase, meaning 'it does not follow,' names the logical fallacy in which a conclusion is not supported by its premises — and informally describes any irrelevant remark dropped into conversation.

citations.title: Formal fallacy
citations.snippet: In logic and philosophy, a formal fallacy is a pattern of reasoning with a flaw in its logical structure. A formal fallacy is contrasted with an informal fallacy. A formal fallacy must have an invalid logical form and thus be unsound. An in

questions.clue: This English verb, meaning to torment someone with something just out of reach, traces to a Greek figure condemned to stand in a pool of water beneath fruit trees whose branches forever withdrew as he reached upward.

citations.title: Tantalus
citations.snippet: Tantalus, also called Atys, was a Greek mythological figure, most famous for his punishment in Tartarus: for either revealing many secrets of the gods, for stealing ambrosia from them, or for trying to trick them into eating his son, he was

questions.clue: Like a signed but otherwise blank cheque, this French expression describes a situation in which an agreement is left open-ended — or a party is willing to consider any expense in the pursuance of their goals.

citations.title: Blank cheque
citations.snippet: A blank cheque or blank check in the literal sense is a cheque that has no monetary value written in, but is already signed. In the figurative sense, it is used to describe a situation in which an agreement has been made that is open-ended

questions.clue: Old Norse warriors who reportedly fought in a trance-like fury on the battlefield gave this modern adjective for wild, uncontrolled rage its name.

citations.title: Berserker
citations.snippet: In the Old Norse written corpus, berserkers were Scandinavian warriors who were said to have fought in a trance-like fury, a characteristic which later gave rise to the modern English adjective berserk 'furiously violent or out of control'.

questions.clue: This French phrase for the final, merciful blow administered to a mortally wounded person or animal has passed into English to describe any decisive finishing stroke that conclusively ends a contest.

citations.title: Coup de grâce
citations.snippet: A coup de grâce is an act of mercy killing in which a mortally wounded person or animal is fatally struck with a melee weapon or shot with a projectile to kill them quickly and end their suffering, with or without their consent. With animal

questions.clue: Coined in 1944 by a Texas congressman, this word for incomprehensible bureaucratic jargon owes its existence to that politician's grandfather — a rancher whose habit of leaving his cattle unmarked gave English its word for a nonconformist.

citations.title: Samuel Maverick
citations.snippet: Samuel Augustus Maverick was a Texas lawyer, politician, land baron and signer of the Texas Declaration of Independence. His name is the source of the term "maverick", first attested in 1867. He was the grandfather of Texas politician Maury
